title: Designing a distributed rate limiter for the API gateway
goal: Design a distributed rate limiter for the API gateway that tolerates bursts, stays accurate, holds p99 latency, and works multi-region.

branches.reason: root planning line and final convergence
algorithm exploration: token bucket vs sliding-window log vs sliding-window counter
storage and coordination angle pursued in parallel: redis vs local plus gossip
verify candidate designs against requirements: burst, accuracy, p99, multi-region
revisit the stashed sliding-window-counter approach and converge

units.full: Okay, the task is a distributed rate limiter sitting in front of our API gateway. Before I touch any algorithm I need to be honest about what actually matters here. We run multiple gateway instances across regions, so this is not a single-node counter problem; it is a coordination problem dressed up as a counting problem. The hard requirements I keep coming back to are: tolerate legitimate bursts without punishing good clients, stay accurate enough that we do not over- or under-admit by a wide margin, keep the limiter off the p99 critical path, and behave sanely across regions. Let me hold those four as my acceptance criteria and explore algorithm and storage as two separate threads.
units.thought: Frame the rate limiter as a distributed coordination problem with four acceptance criteria: burst tolerance, accuracy, low p99, and multi-region behavior.

units.full: There are really two independent design axes I should pull apart so I do not conflate them. One axis is the counting algorithm: how do I decide, given a request history, whether to admit or reject this request right now. The other axis is the storage and coordination substrate: where does the counter state live, who can read and write it atomically, and how do distributed nodes agree. These interact, but I will get cleaner thinking if I explore the algorithm choice first in isolation, then explore the storage substrate in parallel, and only at the end check which combinations actually satisfy all four requirements together.
units.thought: Split the problem into two independent axes to explore in parallel: the counting algorithm, and the storage/coordination substrate.

units.full: Let me start on the algorithm axis with the classic candidates. Three are worth weighing: token bucket, sliding-window log, and sliding-window counter. Token bucket maintains a bucket that refills at a steady rate and drains one token per request; it is cheap and naturally allows bursts up to the bucket capacity. Sliding-window log keeps a timestamp per request and counts how many fall inside the trailing window; it is exact but memory-hungry. Sliding-window counter is the hybrid: it keeps two fixed-window counters and interpolates between them, trading a little accuracy for tiny constant memory. I will think each through against burst tolerance and accuracy before judging cost.
units.thought: Enumerate three algorithm candidates: token bucket, sliding-window log, and sliding-window counter, to weigh against burst tolerance, accuracy, and memory cost.

units.full: Token bucket first. The mental model is a bucket of capacity B that refills r tokens per second; each request costs one token, and you admit only if a token is available. The thing I like is that bursts are a first-class feature: a client that was idle accumulates up to B tokens and can spend them in a quick burst, which is exactly the legitimate-burst behavior we want to tolerate. The state per client is tiny: just a token count and a last-refill timestamp. The refill is lazy, computed on read from elapsed time, so there is no background sweeper. The catch is that B and r are two knobs, and reasoning about the effective long-run rate versus instantaneous burst takes care.
units.thought: Token bucket gives first-class burst tolerance via accumulated tokens, with tiny per-client state (token count plus last-refill timestamp) and lazy refill on read.

units.full: Now sliding-window log. Here I store the actual timestamp of every request in the trailing window, then on each new request I evict timestamps older than the window and count what remains. The appeal is that it is exactly accurate: there is no boundary artifact, no interpolation, the count is the literal number of requests in the last N seconds. The problem is cost. Memory grows with the request rate times the window, so a hot client at thousands of requests per second over a minute window is storing tens of thousands of timestamps. Every request also does an eviction scan or a sorted-set range delete. For a high-throughput gateway this is the most accurate and the most expensive option, and the cost lands right on the request path.
units.thought: Sliding-window log is exactly accurate with no boundary artifact, but memory and per-request eviction cost scale with request rate times window, hitting the hot request path.

units.full: Sliding-window counter is the hybrid I half-remember being the practical sweet spot. Instead of per-request timestamps, keep a counter for the current fixed window and one for the previous fixed window. The estimated count is the current-window count plus the previous-window count weighted by how far the sliding window still overlaps the previous fixed window. So if we are thirty percent into the current minute, you take all of this minute plus seventy percent of last minute. Memory is two integers per client, constant. Accuracy is approximate but the error is bounded and small under smooth traffic, and it has no unbounded eviction scan. This looks like the accuracy-versus-cost compromise the gateway actually wants.
units.thought: Sliding-window counter keeps two fixed-window counters and interpolates by overlap, giving constant two-integer memory with small bounded error, no eviction scan.

units.full: One subtlety with the sliding-window counter I should flag to myself before I forget it. The interpolation assumes the previous window's requests were spread uniformly across that window. When traffic is bursty rather than smooth, that assumption is exactly wrong: a burst concentrated at the very end of the previous window gets under-counted as we slide past it, and a burst at the start gets over-counted. So the very approximation that makes it cheap is weakest precisely in the burst regime we care most about tolerating. I am stashing this concern rather than resolving it now, because the storage axis may change which algorithm is even viable, but I want to come back and verify the burst accuracy explicitly.
units.thought: Stash a concern: sliding-window-counter interpolation assumes uniform spread, so it mis-counts exactly under bursty traffic, the regime we most care about. Revisit later.

units.full: Stepping back on the algorithm axis with what I have. Sliding-window log is out for a high-throughput gateway; the per-request eviction and unbounded memory put cost on the hot path, which directly threatens p99. That leaves token bucket and sliding-window counter as the live candidates. Token bucket is the cleanest for bursts and has the smallest, most predictable per-request work. Sliding-window counter is appealing for its smoothness and tight rate enforcement but carries the burst-accuracy worry I just stashed. My tentative lean is token bucket as the primary, but I am explicitly not closing the sliding-window counter; I will park it and let the storage exploration inform the final call.
units.thought: Drop sliding-window log for cost; keep token bucket as the tentative primary and park sliding-window counter as a live alternative pending the storage exploration.

units.full: Switching to the storage and coordination axis now, independent of which algorithm wins. The core tension is that the counter state is shared across many gateway instances, possibly across regions, and every request needs to read-modify-write it atomically. The two broad shapes are centralized and decentralized. Centralized means a shared store, almost certainly Redis, that every gateway node talks to for each decision. Decentralized means each node keeps a local counter and the nodes reconcile asynchronously, for example via gossip or periodic flushes to a shared aggregate. Centralized is accurate but adds a network hop per request; decentralized is fast but only approximately consistent. Let me work each through.
units.thought: On the storage axis the tension is atomic shared counter state: centralized via Redis with a network hop per request, versus decentralized local counters reconciled by gossip.

units.full: Centralized Redis first. Every gateway node, on each request, runs an atomic operation against Redis to update and check the counter. The big win is a single source of truth: all nodes see the same count, so the limit is enforced globally and accurately regardless of which node a client hits. Redis gives me the atomicity I need cheaply: INCR with EXPIRE for fixed windows, or a small Lua script to do the whole token-bucket refill-and-decrement atomically so there is no read-then-write race between nodes. The cost is a network round trip to Redis on the critical path of every request, plus Redis becomes a throughput bottleneck and a failure domain I must plan around.
units.thought: Centralized Redis is a single source of truth with cheap atomicity (INCR/EXPIRE or a Lua refill-and-decrement script), but adds a round trip per request and a central bottleneck.

units.full: I have to interrogate that per-request Redis round trip against the p99 requirement, because that is where centralized designs usually die. If Redis is in the same datacenter, the round trip is sub-millisecond and probably acceptable as a fraction of total request latency. But the moment a gateway node in another region has to reach a Redis in the primary region, that round trip is tens of milliseconds added to every single request, which will wreck p99 for that region. So centralized Redis is only viable within a region. The atomicity story is great; the latency story is the constraint. This pushes me toward a Redis-per-region topology rather than one global Redis, which then reopens the cross-region consistency question.
units.thought: A per-request Redis round trip is fine intra-region for p99 but cross-region adds tens of ms, so centralized Redis is only viable per-region, reopening cross-region consistency.

units.full: Now the decentralized shape: each gateway node keeps its own local in-memory counter and admits against a per-node share of the global limit, then nodes reconcile asynchronously. The reconciliation could be gossip between nodes or periodic flushes to a shared aggregate that redistributes headroom. The appeal is obvious: the admit decision is a local memory operation with zero network hops, so p99 is excellent and there is no central bottleneck or single failure domain. The price is accuracy. Between reconciliation rounds, nodes have stale views, so the global limit can be overshot when traffic suddenly concentrates on a few nodes, or under-utilized when a node holds headroom it is not using. It trades exactness for latency and availability.
units.thought: Decentralized local counters reconciled by gossip or periodic flush give zero-hop local admit and great p99, but stale views overshoot or under-utilize the global limit.

units.full: Clock skew is the quiet landmine across both shapes, and I want to note it before it bites me in verification. Every windowed or token-bucket algorithm depends on time: window boundaries, refill amounts computed from elapsed time, eviction cutoffs. If node A's clock runs ahead of node B's, then in a decentralized design they disagree about which window a request belongs to and about how many tokens to refill, which directly corrupts the count. Even with centralized Redis, if I let each gateway node pass its own timestamp into the Lua script, skew leaks in; it is safer to take the time authority from Redis itself, or from a tightly NTP-synced source, so all decisions share one clock. Clock discipline is not optional, it is a correctness dependency.
units.thought: Flag clock skew as a correctness dependency: windows and token refill depend on time, so decisions must share one clock authority (Redis server time or tight NTP), not per-node clocks.

units.full: Synthesizing the storage axis. Pure global-centralized is out on multi-region latency. Pure decentralized is fast but too loose on accuracy to enforce a hard global limit. The shape that keeps surfacing is a hybrid topology: a Redis per region as the regional source of truth for atomic per-region accounting, with gateway nodes in that region doing a fast intra-region round trip, and a looser asynchronous cross-region reconciliation that allocates each region a share of the global budget and adjusts it slowly. That gives intra-region accuracy and acceptable p99, while accepting eventual, approximate cross-region consistency. Time authority comes from the regional Redis to neutralize clock skew within a region.
units.thought: Converge the storage axis on a hybrid: Redis-per-region as regional source of truth with async cross-region budget reconciliation, time authority from Redis to neutralize skew.

units.full: Time to verify the leading combination against my four acceptance criteria explicitly, rather than trusting my gut. The candidate is token bucket as the algorithm, implemented as an atomic Lua refill-and-decrement on a Redis-per-region, with async cross-region budget sharing. Criterion one, burst tolerance: token bucket handles this natively through accumulated tokens, so a strong pass. Criterion three, p99: the only hop is intra-region Redis, sub-millisecond, so this passes as long as I never reach across regions on the request path. Let me handle accuracy and multi-region as their own checks next, since those are where the design is most likely to crack.
units.thought: Verify token-bucket-on-Redis-per-region: burst tolerance passes natively, p99 passes via intra-region Redis only; accuracy and multi-region still need dedicated checks.

units.full: Checking accuracy and multi-region together, because they are entangled. Intra-region, token bucket on a single regional Redis is exact: one source of truth, atomic decrement, no approximation, so per-region accounting is accurate. The accuracy hole is cross-region. If I statically split a global limit of N into N over K per region, a client whose traffic shifts heavily to one region gets throttled there while other regions sit on unused headroom, so the effective global behavior is wrong even though each region is locally exact. So the design is accurate locally but its global accuracy depends entirely on how well the async reconciliation redistributes the budget. That reconciliation quality is now the weakest point in the whole design and deserves a hard look.
units.thought: Token bucket on regional Redis is locally exact, but a static cross-region budget split mis-throttles when traffic shifts, so global accuracy hinges on the async reconciliation quality.

units.full: And here is the new constraint that just landed and changes the calculus: the product team confirms that for several high-value endpoints the limit is a hard contractual ceiling, not a soft target. We are billing and SLA-committing on these, so overshooting the global limit is a compliance problem, not just inefficiency. That kills the comfortable hand-wave I was making about decentralized and async reconciliation being good enough. A token bucket that is locally exact but globally loose because budget redistribution lags is exactly the failure I can no longer accept on those endpoints. I need a path to tight global enforcement, at least for the hard-ceiling tier, and that forces me to reconsider approaches I had set aside.
units.thought: New constraint: certain endpoints have a hard contractual global ceiling, so async budget overshoot is now a compliance failure, forcing tight global enforcement and reconsidering parked approaches.

units.full: Given a hard global ceiling, let me re-examine what token bucket actually buys me there and where it strains. Token bucket's strength was burst tolerance via accumulated tokens, but a hard ceiling is in tension with generous bursting in the first place, since a big saved-up burst is precisely what blows past a strict cap. And enforcing one global token bucket strictly means every request, including cross-region, contends on a single shared bucket, which reintroduces the cross-region round trip I rejected for p99. So token bucket under a hard global ceiling loses its main advantage and reintroduces the latency problem. The clean separation I liked is breaking down; I should revisit the alternative I parked rather than force this one.
units.thought: Under a hard global ceiling, token bucket's burst advantage works against the cap and strict global enforcement reintroduces the cross-region hop, so revisit the parked alternative.

units.full: Revisiting the sliding-window counter I stashed back when I was on the algorithm axis. The reason I parked it was the burst-accuracy worry from the uniform-spread assumption, plus a tentative lean toward token bucket. But the hard-ceiling constraint reweights everything. Sliding-window counter enforces a steadier rate with no large accumulated burst, which is actually what a hard contractual ceiling wants: it naturally resists the big saved-up burst that would blow the cap. Its two-integer constant memory is still cheap, and its approximation error is bounded and small. The very property that made it less attractive for generous bursting, its tighter rate enforcement, is the property the hard-ceiling tier needs. I am pulling it back to the front for that tier.
units.thought: Revisit the parked sliding-window counter: its tight rate enforcement and resistance to large accumulated bursts are exactly what the hard-ceiling tier needs, with cheap constant memory.

units.full: But I must honor the burst-accuracy concern I explicitly stashed earlier rather than just wishing it away. The worry was that sliding-window counter's interpolation assumes uniform spread, so a burst at a window edge is mis-counted. For the hard-ceiling tier, which way does that error cut? Under-counting risks admitting over the ceiling, which is the compliance failure I cannot have; over-counting only rejects a little early, which is merely slightly conservative. So I should bias the interpolation to be conservative, rounding the estimate up rather than down near boundaries, accepting a touch of over-rejection to guarantee I never silently exceed the contractual cap. That turns the algorithm's weakness into a tunable safety margin on the side that matters.
units.thought: Address the stashed interpolation concern: bias sliding-window-counter estimates conservatively (round up near boundaries) so the hard-ceiling tier never under-counts and overshoots the cap.

units.full: Now the cross-region question for the hard-ceiling tier specifically, because that is the unsolved core. A hard global cap genuinely needs global agreement, and the only way to get exactness is a single authoritative counter, which costs a cross-region hop that hurts p99. The honest resolution is to scope the cost: only the hard-ceiling endpoints pay for strict global enforcement, and there are few of them and they are higher-latency-tolerant by nature. So those few endpoints use a single global Redis as the authority for the sliding-window counter, eating the cross-region round trip deliberately, while the vast majority of normal endpoints keep the fast Redis-per-region token bucket with async reconciliation. Tiering the design by endpoint strictness lets each tier pay only the cost it must.
units.thought: Resolve cross-region for the hard-ceiling tier by scoping cost: those few endpoints use a single global Redis authority and accept the cross-region hop, normal endpoints keep per-region token bucket.

units.full: Re-verifying p99 under this tiered design, because I just deliberately added a cross-region hop and that is exactly what I swore to keep off the path. The key is that the hop only applies to the hard-ceiling tier, which is a small minority of traffic and contractually tolerant of slightly higher latency. The overwhelming majority of requests stay on the intra-region token-bucket fast path with a sub-millisecond Redis hop, so the gateway's overall p99 across all traffic is dominated by that fast path and stays healthy. I should still set an explicit per-tier latency budget and alert if the hard-ceiling tier's share of traffic grows, since that would shift the latency profile. Tiering protects p99 by construction as long as the strict tier stays small.
units.thought: Re-verify p99: the cross-region hop is confined to the small hard-ceiling tier, so overall p99 stays dominated by the intra-region fast path; alert if that tier's traffic share grows.

units.full: A naming and observability aside while it is in my head, unrelated to the core algorithm decision. Whatever we ship, the rejection responses need to be clear to clients: a 429 with a Retry-After header and a header exposing the remaining quota and the window reset time, so well-behaved clients can self-throttle instead of hammering us. We should also emit metrics per endpoint and per region for admit and reject counts so we can actually see the limiter working and catch misconfiguration. None of this changes the algorithm choice, but a rate limiter that rejects opaquely just converts one operational problem into a noisier one. I will note it as a requirement for the implementation phase and move on.
units.thought: Aside on client ergonomics: return 429 with Retry-After and remaining-quota headers, and emit per-endpoint per-region admit/reject metrics, so clients self-throttle and we can observe the limiter.

units.full: Let me also sanity-check failure modes, since a limiter that breaks the gateway when its store hiccups is worse than no limiter. If the regional Redis is briefly unreachable, the token-bucket fast-path tier should fail open with a conservative local fallback rate, because blocking all traffic on a counting-store blip is a worse outcome than briefly admitting a bit over. The hard-ceiling tier is the opposite: if its global Redis authority is unreachable, it must fail closed for the contractual endpoints, because admitting past a hard cap is the explicit thing we cannot do. So failure policy itself is tiered: normal tier fails open, hard-ceiling tier fails closed. That asymmetry falls straight out of what each tier is actually protecting.
units.thought: Tier the failure policy: normal token-bucket tier fails open with a conservative local fallback on Redis loss, hard-ceiling tier fails closed since admitting past a hard cap is unacceptable.

units.full: Converging on the final design and writing it down as one coherent shape. It is a tiered distributed rate limiter. Tier one, the default for the vast majority of endpoints, is a token bucket implemented as an atomic Lua refill-and-decrement on a Redis-per-region, giving native burst tolerance, intra-region accuracy, a sub-millisecond hop, and async cross-region budget reconciliation; on regional Redis loss it fails open to a conservative local rate. Tier two, for the few hard-ceiling contractual endpoints, is a conservatively-rounded sliding-window counter on a single global Redis authority, accepting one cross-region hop and failing closed, to guarantee the global cap is never exceeded. Time authority comes from Redis to neutralize clock skew. This satisfies all four criteria by letting each tier pay only the cost its guarantee requires.
units.thought: Converge on a tiered limiter: token bucket on Redis-per-region (fail open) as default, plus conservative sliding-window counter on global Redis (fail closed) for hard-ceiling endpoints, time from Redis.

units.full: Final pass against the four acceptance criteria to make sure the converged design actually clears each, not just feels right. Burst tolerance: the default token-bucket tier handles legitimate bursts natively, and the hard-ceiling tier intentionally limits bursts, which is correct for a contractual cap. Accuracy: locally exact per region everywhere, and globally exact for the hard-ceiling tier via the single authority, with async reconciliation handling the soft tier well enough since it has no compliance requirement. p99: the strict cross-region hop is confined to a small tier, so overall p99 stays dominated by the intra-region fast path. Multi-region: handled by the per-region topology plus the scoped global authority. All four pass with the costs explicitly placed where each guarantee demands them.
units.thought: Final verification against all four criteria: burst tolerance, accuracy, p99, and multi-region each pass, with the tiered design placing each cost only where its guarantee requires it.

units.full: Implementation order so this lands safely rather than all at once. First ship the default token-bucket tier on Redis-per-region with the atomic Lua script and the fail-open fallback, since that covers nearly all traffic and is the lower-risk piece; gate it behind a flag and validate p99 in production with shadow counting before enforcing. Second, layer in the per-endpoint configuration so an endpoint can be tagged hard-ceiling. Third, build the global-Redis sliding-window-counter authority for that tier with conservative rounding and fail-closed behavior, and roll it to one contractual endpoint first to validate the cross-region latency budget before extending. The async cross-region budget reconciliation for the soft tier comes last, since the static split is a safe interim.
units.thought: Stage implementation: ship token-bucket-on-Redis-per-region first behind a flag with shadow counting, then per-endpoint tagging, then the global sliding-window-counter hard-ceiling tier, reconciliation last.

units.full: One thing I want to record as a known limitation rather than pretend I solved it: the async cross-region budget reconciliation for the soft tier is genuinely approximate, and under rapid traffic shifts between regions it will transiently mis-allocate headroom, briefly over-throttling one region while another sits idle. That is acceptable for the soft tier by definition, but it is a real behavior the on-call team should expect and not mistake for a bug. The honest framing is that we bought tight guarantees exactly where the contract demands them and accepted bounded looseness everywhere else, and the soft-tier reconciliation lag is where that looseness shows up. I will document the expected magnitude and the alert threshold so it is a known, bounded behavior rather than a surprise.
units.thought: Record a known limitation: soft-tier async cross-region reconciliation transiently mis-allocates headroom under rapid traffic shifts; bounded and acceptable by design, documented so it is not mistaken for a bug.

units.full: Last, I want to confirm the clock-skew handling holds across the whole converged design, since it threads through both tiers and a missed skew bug corrupts counts silently. The default tier takes its time from the regional Redis inside the Lua script, so all nodes in a region share one clock for window boundaries and token refill, which removes per-node skew within a region. The hard-ceiling tier takes time from its single global Redis authority, so its sliding-window boundaries are computed against one clock globally. In both cases the gateway nodes never use their own wall clocks for rate decisions; the store is the time authority. With that, skew cannot quietly corrupt either tier's accounting, closing the last correctness gap I flagged.
units.thought: Confirm clock-skew handling end to end: both tiers take time from their Redis authority (regional and global) for windows and refill, so gateway nodes never use local clocks for rate decisions.

units.full: I am calling the design done. The converged answer is a two-tier distributed rate limiter: a default fast tier of per-region token buckets on Redis with atomic Lua refill-decrement, native burst tolerance, sub-millisecond hops, fail-open fallback, and async cross-region budget reconciliation; and a strict tier of conservatively-rounded global sliding-window counters on a single Redis authority, fail-closed, for the few hard-ceiling contractual endpoints. Redis is the time authority in both tiers to kill clock skew. It satisfies burst tolerance, accuracy, p99, and multi-region by paying each cost only where its guarantee demands it, with a staged rollout and one documented bounded limitation in soft-tier reconciliation. This is a design I would defend in review.
units.thought: Final decision: a two-tier limiter, per-region token buckets (fail open) plus global conservative sliding-window counters (fail closed) for hard-ceiling endpoints, Redis as time authority, staged rollout.

probes.why: Focusing on the decision to revisit the parked sliding-window counter (#18) should pull back the original sliding-window-counter exploration (#5, branch 1, ~13 ticks earlier) and the stashed uniform-spread/interpolation concern (#6) that share its entities; the recent hard-ceiling observation (#16) is adjacent in time but is about the constraint, not the algorithm, and should fade.

Focusing on honoring the burst-accuracy concern (#19, interpolation/conservative-rounding) should resurface the exact stashed concern from #6 (interpolation, uniform-assumption, ~13 ticks earlier, different branch) it is answering; the recent #16 and #17 are about the hard-ceiling constraint and token bucket, not the interpolation worry, and should fade.

Focusing on the cross-region authority decision for the hard-ceiling tier (#20, global-redis/cross-region) should pull back the original centralized-Redis exploration (#9) and the p99 cross-region verification (#10) from branch 2 (~10 ticks earlier) that share redis/cross-region/p99 entities; the recent 429/observability aside (#22) is unrelated and should fade.

Focusing on confirming clock-skew handling (#28) should resurface the original clock-skew flag (#12, branch 2, ~16 ticks earlier) sharing clock-skew/ntp/redis/time-authority entities; the recent reconciliation known-limitation note (#27) is about headroom allocation, not time, and should fade.

Focusing on the final four-criteria verification (#25, burst/accuracy/p99/multi-region) should pull back the first four-criteria verification (#14, branch 3, ~11 ticks earlier) that shares the same acceptance-criteria entities; the recent client-ergonomics aside (#22) does not bear on the criteria check and should fade.
